section.title: God's Love for Israel
section.sectionRationale: After chapters of accumulating judgment, the tone breaks open into the tenderest revelation of God's heart in the book. God remembers Israel as a beloved child He taught to walk, and though justice demands ruin, His compassion recoils within Him. Here is the wounded, pursuing love of God, and the words Matthew will apply to Christ.
section.thesis: Though Israel spurned the Father who called His son out of Egypt and taught him to walk, God's compassion overrides His wrath, for He is God and not man, the Holy One who will not give His people up.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the Father's remembered love for His child; the child's stubborn turning away; and the divine heart that recoils, refusing to give Ephraim up.

units.label: Out of Egypt I called My son
units.note: the Father who taught him to walk

verses.text: When Israel was a child, I loved him, and out of Egypt I called My {{ben|son}}.
But the more I called Israel, the farther they departed from Me. They sacrificed to the Baals and burned incense to carved images.
It was I who taught Ephraim to walk, taking them by the arms, but they never realized that it was I who healed them.
I led them with cords of kindness, with ropes of {{ahavah|love}}; I lifted the yoke from their necks and bent down to feed them.

units.label: The child who would not return

verses.text: Will they not return to the land of Egypt and be ruled by Assyria because they refused to repent?
A sword will flash through their cities; it will destroy the bars of their gates and consume them in their own plans.
My people are bent on turning from Me. Though they call to the Most High, He will by no means exalt them.

units.label: How can I give you up?

verses.text: How could I give you up, O Ephraim? How could I surrender you, O Israel? How could I make you like Admah? How could I treat you like Zeboiim? My heart is turned within Me; My {{nicham|compassion}} is stirred!
I will not execute the full fury of My anger; I will not destroy Ephraim again. For I am God and not man, the Holy One among you, and I will not come in wrath.
They will walk after the LORD; He will roar like a lion. When He roars, His children will come trembling from the west.
They will come trembling like birds from Egypt and like doves from the land of Assyria. Then I will settle them in their homes, declares the LORD.
Ephraim surrounds Me with lies, the house of Israel with deceit; but Judah still walks with God and is faithful to the Holy One.

ben.gloss: A son, a child. God calls Israel His son whom He called out of Egypt, framing the exodus as the rescue of a beloved child. Matthew 2:15 applies this very phrase to Jesus, the true and faithful Son, showing that Israel's story is taken up and fulfilled in Christ.

ahavah.gloss: Love, affection. Here it is the fatherly, tender love with which God led Israel, with cords of kindness and ropes of love, lifting the yoke and stooping to feed His child. It reveals that God's dealings with His people flow from deep affection, not cold obligation.

nicham.gloss: To be moved to compassion, to relent, to have one's heart change toward mercy. In verse 8 God's compassion is stirred, His heart recoils within Him, and He turns from executing the full fury of His wrath. It expresses the astonishing depth of divine mercy that overrides deserved judgment.

basic.context: The thunder of judgment gives way to the tenderest words in Hosea. God remembers Israel as a little child He loved and called out of Egypt, whom He taught to walk, holding him by the arms and bending down to feed him. But the child kept running away to idols. Justice would rightly destroy them as it destroyed Admah and Zeboiim near Sodom. Yet God cries out, How can I give you up? His heart recoils within Him, His compassion is stirred, and He declares He will not carry out the full fury of His anger, for He is God and not man.
basic.hermeneutics: This is deeply anthropomorphic poetry, portraying God's inner life in human terms so we can grasp the reality of His love and mercy. Verse 9, I am God and not man, guards against misreading: God's mercy is not fickle human sentiment but the steadfast, holy love of God Himself. Matthew 2:15 reads verse 1 as fulfilled in Christ, the true Israel and Son.
basic.originalLanguages: God calls Israel His ben, His son, called out of Egypt, a phrase Matthew applies to Jesus. He led them with ahavah, tender love, cords of kindness. And at the climax His nicham, compassion, is stirred so that His heart recoils from wrath. Son, love, and relenting compassion together open the very heart of God.
basic.typology: Out of Egypt I called My son is directly fulfilled in Christ, whom Matthew shows recapitulating and redeeming Israel's story. Where the son Israel was faithless, Jesus the true Son is faithful. God's refusal to give His people up, absorbing rather than executing the full fury of wrath, foreshadows the cross, where wrath and mercy meet and God saves His people at cost to Himself.
basic.christ: Matthew takes the words out of Egypt I called My son and applies them to Jesus, the true Son who succeeds where Israel failed and so redeems His people. The mystery of verse 8, God's compassion overriding His wrath, is not sentimentality; it finds its resolution at the cross. There God does not simply set His justice aside; He satisfies it in His Son, so that He can righteously refuse to give His people up. The Holy One among you who will not come in wrath is the God who comes in Christ to bear the wrath Himself.
basic.god: This chapter reveals the heart of God more nakedly than almost any other. He is a Father whose love goes back to His people's infancy, who is genuinely grieved by their rebellion, and whose compassion recoils at the thought of their ruin. He is holy, and precisely because He is God and not man His mercy is deeper and more constant than any human love. His love does not ignore sin, but it will not let His people go.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 2:15 applies out of Egypt I called My son to Christ. Romans 5 shows God demonstrating His love in Christ's death for sinners. Lamentations 3 and Deuteronomy 29:23 illuminate God's compassions and the fate of Admah and Zeboiim.
basic.oneStory: Chapter 11 unveils the Father heart of God, who called His son out of Egypt and cannot bear to give His people up. The whole Bible answers this in Christ, the true Son called out of Egypt, in whom God's wrath is borne and His compassion triumphs, so that His people are settled home forever.

context.literary: A lyric of divine love that shifts from remembered tenderness to grief to the astonishing self-questioning of verse 8. It is structured around God as Father and Israel as wayward child, and it climaxes not in judgment but in mercy, a rare and stunning turn among the judgment oracles.
context.historical: The threat of returning to Egypt and being ruled by Assyria reflects the looming exile. Admah and Zeboiim were cities destroyed with Sodom and Gomorrah, so the comparison evokes total, deserved destruction, which makes God's restraint all the more striking.
context.canonical: Verse 1 is quoted in Matthew 2:15 as fulfilled in Jesus, making this chapter a key link between Israel's exodus and Christ. The revelation of God as compassionate Father anticipates the fatherhood of God in the teaching of Jesus and the parable of the prodigal son.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal that beneath the just judgments of the previous chapters beats the heart of a loving Father whose compassion will not finally give His people up, so that Israel might know both the seriousness of their sin and the depth of God's mercy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter chiefly reveals God's own heart and actions rather than commanding ours. Its implicit call is to return to such a Father, and its enduring truth is the character of God as holy, loving, and merciful.

debates.question: What does it mean that God's compassion overrides His wrath in verses 8 and 9?

views.label: Mercy triumphing without denying justice
views.case: God restrains the full fury of wrath and preserves a people, yet the tension is only fully resolved when His justice is satisfied at the cross, so mercy triumphs over judgment without setting justice aside.

views.label: The revelation of God's unchanging character
views.case: Because He is God and not man, His covenant love is constant; His relenting is not a change of mind but the outworking of who He has always been toward His people.

debates.bearing: Both together show that God's mercy is neither weak nor arbitrary; it is the holy love of God that finds its righteous ground in Christ, who bears the wrath so the Father can keep His child.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter opens the heart of God through three words. He calls Israel His ben, His son, loved and called out of Egypt, the very phrase Matthew applies to Jesus. He describes leading this child with ahavah, tender love, with cords of kindness and ropes of love, lifting the yoke and bending down to feed him. And at the climax, when justice demands the fate of Admah and Zeboiim, God's nicham, His compassion, is stirred, His heart recoils within Him, and He will not execute the full fury of His anger. For He is God and not man, the Holy One among His people, and it is precisely His holiness that makes His mercy so unlike fickle human relenting.

typology.type: Out of Egypt I called My son
typology.fulfillment: Christ the true Son, whom Matthew shows recapitulating and redeeming Israel's story, Matthew 2:15.

typology.type: God who will not give His people up though justice demands it
typology.fulfillment: Christ crucified, where God satisfies wrath in Himself so He can righteously keep His people, Romans 3:25-26.

typology.type: The Holy One among you who will not come in wrath
typology.fulfillment: Christ, God with us, who comes to bear the wrath rather than pour it out on His people, Romans 5:9.

study.christ: When Matthew writes that out of Egypt I called My son was fulfilled in Jesus, he reveals that Christ is the true Israel, the faithful Son who obeys where the first son rebelled, and so redeems the people. The heart-piercing question of verse 8, How can I give you up, and the compassion that overrides wrath, are not divine sentimentality; they are answered at the cross. There God does not merely waive His justice; He pours out its full fury on His own Son, so that He may righteously refuse to give up His people. The Holy One among you who says I will not come in wrath is the God who comes in Christ to absorb that wrath Himself, and then to settle His trembling children safely home.
study.god: Here the character of God is laid bare as nowhere else in Hosea. He is a Father whose love reaches back to His people's infancy, who taught them to walk and stooped to feed them, and who is truly grieved when they run away. His compassion is so deep that it recoils at the thought of their destruction, even the destruction their sin deserves. And He tells us why this mercy is trustworthy: because He is God and not man, His love is not fickle but holy and constant. This is a God whose holiness intensifies rather than cancels His mercy, and who will not let His people go.

crossReferences.note: Out of Egypt I called My son is fulfilled in Christ, the true and faithful Son.
God shows His love in that Christ died for us and saves us from wrath, the mercy of Hosea 11 grounded in the cross.
The father runs to the returning prodigal, the same Father heart Hosea reveals.
Admah and Zeboiim, destroyed with Sodom, show the ruin God's compassion holds back from Israel.

study.oneStory: Chapter 11 unveils the Father heart of God, who called His son out of Egypt and cannot bear to give His people up. The whole Bible answers this in Christ, the true Son called out of Egypt, in whom the full fury of wrath is borne and God's compassion triumphs, so that His trembling children are settled home forever.

quiz.q: How does God remember His relationship with Israel in verses 1 to 4?

options.text: As a father who loved a child, called him out of Egypt, taught him to walk, and bent down to feed him.
options.note: Verses 1 to 4 use tender fatherly images of raising a child.

options.text: As a distant ruler with no affection for Israel.
options.note: The whole point is God's deep fatherly love, not distance.

options.text: As an employer paying wages for work done.
options.note: The relationship is family love, not a transaction.

options.text: As a stranger who barely knew them.
options.note: God knew and nurtured Israel from infancy.

quiz.q: What happens in God's heart in verse 8?

options.text: His compassion is stirred and His heart recoils, so He will not carry out the full fury of His wrath.
options.note: Verse 8 reveals compassion overriding deserved judgment.

options.text: He grows even more determined to destroy them utterly.
options.note: The opposite; His compassion holds back the full fury.

options.text: He becomes indifferent to Israel's fate.
options.note: He is anything but indifferent; His heart is deeply moved.

options.text: He decides sin does not matter.
options.note: Sin still matters; mercy triumphs without denying justice.

quiz.q: Why does God give for His mercy in verse 9?

options.text: Because He is God and not man, the Holy One among them.
options.note: Verse 9 grounds His constant mercy in His being God, not fickle man.

options.text: Because Israel had finally earned it.
options.note: The mercy flows from God's character, not Israel's merit.

options.text: Because the sin was not really serious.
options.note: The sin was grave; the mercy is greater and rooted in God.

options.text: Because He had forgotten their idolatry.
options.note: He remembers it fully, yet chooses compassion.

quiz.q: How is verse 1 fulfilled in the New Testament?

options.text: Matthew 2:15 applies out of Egypt I called My son to Jesus, the true and faithful Son.
options.note: Christ recapitulates and redeems Israel's story as God's Son.

options.text: It is never referred to in the New Testament.
options.note: Matthew explicitly cites it as fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: It refers only to a future political nation.
options.note: The New Testament applies it to Christ Himself.

options.text: It predicts a return of Israel to literal slavery.
options.note: It is fulfilled in Christ called out of Egypt, not renewed slavery.

quiz.q: How does God's refusal to give His people up point to the cross?

options.text: At the cross God satisfies His justice in His Son, so He can righteously refuse to give His people up.
options.note: Wrath is borne by Christ so mercy can triumph, Romans 3:25 to 26.

options.text: It shows God simply ignores justice when He feels like it.
options.note: God does not ignore justice; He satisfies it in Christ.

options.text: It means no one ever faces judgment.
options.note: Judgment is real; Christ bears it for His people.

options.text: It has no connection to the cross.
options.note: The cross is where wrath and mercy meet, resolving verse 8.

questions: God remembers teaching Israel to walk and stooping to feed him. How does picturing God's fatherly tenderness toward you change the way you relate to Him?
Israel kept running from the God who loved them. Where do you find yourself drifting from God even as He keeps calling you?
God's compassion recoiled at giving His people up. When have you most needed to hear that God will not let you go?
God's mercy is trustworthy because He is God and not man. How does the constancy of God's love steady you when your own love for Him wavers?
The wrath God held back was ultimately borne by Christ. How does the cross deepen your understanding of the words, How can I give you up?

translationNotes.note: Out of Egypt I called My son is cited in Matthew 2:15 as fulfilled in Christ, the true Son of God.
The BSB follows the Septuagint; the Hebrew reads the more they called them, the more they went from them, but the sense of persistent turning away is the same.
The BSB prints em dashes around the Holy One among you; they are rendered here as commas per house style. I will not come in wrath may also be rendered I will not come against their cities.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hosea 11:1-12, with poetic lines joined into running verses. Em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas per house style.
